title: Illicit Consent Grant (Malicious OAuth Application)
description: Response playbook for an illicit-consent-grant / consent-phishing attack, where a user is tricked into authorizing a malicious third-party OAuth application that then reads mail, files, or contacts via API without needing the password again. Triggered by a reported consent prompt, a risky-application alert, or anomalous Graph/API access from an unfamiliar app.
author: Cognis Digital

steps.name: Restrict and review app consent
steps.description: Configure user consent to allow only verified publishers or low-risk scopes, and enable an admin-consent request workflow so risky grants are reviewed before approval.

steps.name: Know how to revoke grants at scale
steps.description: Confirm the IR team can list and revoke OAuth application grants and service principals across the tenant and can disable an enterprise application quickly.

steps.name: Identify the malicious application and its scopes
steps.description: Find the granted application, who consented, and which permissions (mail.read, files.read.all, offline_access) it holds.
steps.detection: Audit logs for 'Consent to application' events and enterprise-application sign-ins; flag apps requesting broad read scopes plus offline_access (refresh tokens).

steps.name: Scope API-driven data access
steps.description: Determine what the app accessed using its tokens since the grant.
steps.detection: API/Graph activity logs attributed to the application's service principal: mail reads, file downloads, and mailbox-rule creation.

steps.name: Revoke the grant and tokens
steps.response: Revoke the application's consent and delete its service principal so existing refresh tokens stop working, then disable the enterprise application tenant-wide.

steps.name: Block the app and contain affected users
steps.response: Add the application ID to a block list, and for users who consented, revoke sessions and review for any inbox rules or grants the app created.

steps.name: Remove residual access
steps.response: Confirm no related service principals, app passwords, or delegated permissions remain, and rotate secrets the app could have reached.

steps.name: Restore normal operations
steps.response: Confirm legitimate applications still function, communicate the revocation to affected users, and monitor consent and enterprise-app sign-in telemetry.

steps.name: Harden the consent surface
steps.description: Tighten user-consent policy, add a detection for high-risk scope grants, and educate users that consent prompts can be as dangerous as password phishing.
